notes.note: The "wickedness" is that of young Quintus, Cicero's nephew, who had denounced his uncle to Caesar to clear his own name; the genitive "Quinti" here is the son, not Cicero's brother. A letter from Atticus had reopened the wound by enclosing the nephew's own accusatory letter.

notes.anchor: Many things are said to be detaining him in Asia, Pharnaces above all
notes.note: Pharnaces II, king of the Bosporus and son of Mithridates VI, had overrun Pontus and defeated a Roman force; subduing him kept Caesar in the East and so prolonged Cicero's wait. Caesar crushed him at Zela in August 47 BC — the victory of his "veni, vidi, vici."

notes.anchor: The twelfth legion, which Sulla approached first
notes.note: The veteran legions in Campania mutinied in 47 BC over unpaid bonuses and discharge; P. Cornelius Sulla, sent to pacify them, was pelted with stones when he tried to address the twelfth. The "Sulla" of this section is the Caesarian commander, not the dictator.

Here "Sulla" is the dictator L. Cornelius Sulla, cited as a historical exemplum: Atticus had pointed to how the partisans of Sulla's day trimmed their conduct to circumstance. Cicero replies that even those expedients were tempered with too little moderation to serve as a model now.
